Title: Malaysia needs clear policy and regulatory direction to maintain investor confidence: Fadillah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia needs clear policy and regulatory direction to maintain investor confidence and support the country's energy transition agenda, Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Fadillah Yusof said on Thursday.
He said that while political leadership may change over time, consistent policies and a stable regulatory framework were crucial in assuring investors that the country remains committed to its long-term development goals.
"New ministers can come in, but more importantly for Malaysia, we need clear policies and clear regulatory perspectives so that the message to investors remains consistent," he said.
Fadillah said this during a Ministerial Session on "Powering Malaysia's Next Growth Chapter: Energy, Economy and Digital In Sync" at the Energy Transition Conference 2026 (ETCon26) here.
He added that government institutions and the civil service play a critical role in ensuring continuity in policy implementation, regardless of changes in ministerial portfolios or political developments.
Fadillah, who is also Energy Transition and Water Transformation Minister, said his ministry would collaborate with other ministries to strengthen energy infrastructure as a strategic national enabler, moving beyond simply providing energy to meet market demand.
He added that Malaysia also needs to adopt a more structured whole-of-government approach to strengthen coordination among ministries and agencies and enhance the country's competitiveness.
While mechanisms already exist to facilitate collaboration across government entities, Fadillah said a more comprehensive and structured approach would be required moving forward.
He said approval processes must be streamlined to improve efficiency and shorten turnaround times, while infrastructure readiness, particularly in terms of energy supply, must keep pace with the country's economic development plans.
"The challenge is how we get alignment not only at the federal level but, more importantly, at the state and municipal council levels," Fadillah said.
Fadillah also stressed the need for Malaysia to strengthen its regulatory and legal framework to support emerging technologies and industries, particularly in addressing challenges posed by artificial intelligence (AI), including deepfake technology.
He said the country must ensure the necessary infrastructure, regulations and legislation are in place to support economic growth and digital transformation.
Fadillah also noted that Malaysia's energy transition efforts must be guided by the energy trilemma of security, sustainability and affordability.
He said Malaysia is targeting renewable energy to comprise 70 per cent of its total electricity generation capacity by 2050.
"The challenge when it comes to renewables, it is intermittent in nature. We need to stabilise the grid. This is where TNB has committed to injecting more than RM40 billion to upgrade the grid system (and), to digitalise all our transmission and systems," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Leverage energy transition as a catalyst for economic restructuring: Akmal Nasrullah
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The energy transition should be leveraged as a catalyst for broader economic restructuring, with strategic focus areas including artificial intelligence (AI), semiconductors and data centres, to enhance productivity and strengthen the country's economic prospects.
Economy Minister Akmal Nasrullah Mohd Nasir said the energy transition agenda should not be viewed solely through the lens of decarbonisation as integration of energy, digitalisation and economic growth is crucial to strengthening Malaysia's resilience and long-term competitiveness.
"We must look beyond treating energy and digitalisation as separate segments. The objective is to integrate these growth drivers from a holistic perspective," he said.
"Therefore, a whole-of-government and whole-of-nation approach is needed in order to further strengthen integration," he said during a Ministerial Session on "Powering Malaysia's Next Growth Chapter: Energy, Economy & Digital In Sync" at the Energy Transition Conference 2026 (ETCon26) on Thursday.
Akmal Nasrullah said Malaysia must place greater emphasis on productivity and value creation as it pursues more integrated economic growth, as energy, digitalisation and economic complexity are closely interconnected.
He said given the growing energy and water demands of digital technologies, energy serves as the foundation for digitalisation as it enhances productivity and drives higher-value economic activities.
"From productivity, we should aim for greater economic complexity and better incomes for our citizens. That is why Malaysia must move beyond sectoral growth and adopt a more holistic approach," he said.
Akmal Nasrullah also urged the nation to maintain a reliable baseload power supply while advancing its energy transition agenda to ensure long-term energy security and grid stability.
He said natural gas would become increasingly important in maintaining energy security and grid stability as Malaysia gradually reduces its reliance on coal.
"We have to ensure the continuity of our gas supply. Currently, we are going through the process of coming up with the National Gas Roadmap (NGR), and hopefully we can deliver it (NGR) this year.
"The closure of the Strait of Hormuz is a way for us to test our concept as the gas market requires a holistic approach in order for Malaysia to remain resilient," he added.
Furthermore, Akmal Nasrullah said Malaysia remains an attractive and lower-risk investment destination, supported by policy stability and consistency.
"Energy transition is not only about decarbonisation, but an opportunity for economic restructuring and moving into higher-value industries.
"Strategic investments should help create skilled jobs, develop local talents and strengthen Malaysia's long-term competitiveness," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sentoria to be suspended, delisted over regularisation failure
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Shares of Main Market-listed Sentoria Group Bhd will be suspended from trading on Bursa Malaysia Securities effective June 12, 2026, ahead of its scheduled delisting four days later after failing to regularise its financial position.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia on Thursday, the diversified property developer and leisure operator said it would be removed from the Official List of Bursa Securities on June 16, 2026, unless it submits an appeal against the delisting by June 11, 2026.
The delisting comes after Sentoria failed to submit its regularisation plan to the Securities Commission Malaysia or Bursa Securities for approval within the extended deadline of June 3, 2026, placing the group in breach of regulatory requirements for financially distressed listed issuers.
"In the event the company submits an appeal to Bursa Securities within the appeal timeframe, the delisting of the company's securities from the Official List of Bursa Securities on June 16, 2026, will be deferred pending a decision on the appeal.
"The securities will be withdrawn from Bursa Depository by the company upon their delisting from the Official List of Bursa Securities," it said.
While the delisting marks the end of Sentoria's status as a publicly traded company, its ability to continue executing property developments and monetising existing assets will likely remain central to any future restructuring and recovery plans.
Sentoria, known for its township and integrated property developments as well as hospitality and theme park operations, has undertaken several residential and mixed-use projects, particularly in Pahang and Johor. Among its flagship developments are the Sentoria Borneo Samariang Resort City in Kuching and the Bukit Gambang Resort City integrated development in Pahang, which combines residential, commercial and tourism-related components.
Following the delisting, Sentoria will continue to exist as an unlisted entity and may proceed with its operational activities, corporate restructuring exercises and business plans, and property development ventures.
The group shareholders would continue to retain their rights and interests under the Companies Act 2016, although their shares would no longer be quoted or traded on Bursa Securities.
The Companies Act 2016 will continue to safeguard the interests and rights of shareholders, it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australian far-right does U-turn on seizing foreigners' homes
Article:
SYDNEY: A top representative of Australia's far-right One Nation party has clarified the party will not be kicking foreign permanent residents out of their homes if it takes power.
An opinion poll last week showed One Nation, for decades a fringe group led by provocateur Pauline Hanson, had become the country's most popular party.
Speaking on Sky News Australia on Thursday evening, One Nation lawmaker Barnaby Joyce said that if it won power the party would force foreign permanent residents to sell their homes or have their property seized by the state.
"Commit yourself totally to this nation and your problems are solved, but if you want to have a foot in two camps then in two camps you'll have your feet," Joyce said.
But he then reportedly reversed tack following calls to One Nation colleagues, asking if he could re-do the answer with the correct policy.
"On further investigation and discussion... no, we are not going to be kicking permanent residents out of their house," he told the broadcaster.
Ex-deputy prime minister Joyce has long been known for colourful outbursts, once threatening to kill Johnny Depp's dogs and being forced to ditch the drink after he was filmed lying across a busy footpath in a stupor.
The rise of One Nation, which advocates for swingeing immigration cuts and campaigns against "radical Islam", has thrown into question a long-held notion that Australia's voting system insulates it from the global rise of populist parties.
Australia's economy minister said this week that "legitimate" concerns over housing and the cost of living were driving support for the party.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan May foreign reserves fall by US$77bil after large interventions
Article:
TOKYO: Japan's foreign reserves fell by US$77 billion in May, government data showed on Friday, as Tokyo resumed massive interventions to stem the yen's slide, fuelling market speculation over how far it's willing to draw on its US$1.3 trillion war chest.
The reserves stood at US$1.306 trillion at the end of May, down from US$1.383 trillion a month earlier, according to the Ministry of Finance (MOF) data.
The data on Japan's foreign reserves, the world's second largest after China, came a week after separate MOF figures showed that Japan spent 11.7 trillion yen (US$73.13 billion) since late April to support the yen in what was the largest-ever intervention ​round in a month.
Investors are scrutinising Japan's foreign reserves and intervention records for clues on how much Japan might be able to spend to rein in the yen's sharp decline against the dollar.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan plans to rebuild ageing nuclear plants to boost power supply
Article:
TOKYO: Japan plans to rebuild between two and five ageing nuclear reactors by the 2040s and as many as 11 to 14 by the 2050s as it aims to secure stable power supply, public broadcaster NHK reported, citing a draft policy to be presented on Friday.
The proposal, to be outlined by the Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry at a meeting on nuclear policy, reflects a shift towards greater reliance on atomic energy to help meet rising power demand and reduce costly fuel imports.
Japan shut its 54 operating nuclear reactors after the 2011 Fukushima nuclear disaster, due to public fear about the industry's safety standards. Of the 33 units that remain operable, 15 have been restarted.
Tokyo last year revised its basic energy policy to maximise the use of nuclear power. However, many reactors are approaching or exceeding their 60-year operational lifespans, raising concerns about a future decline in nuclear capacity even if restarts of idled plants continue.
By setting concrete replacement targets, the government aims to improve predictability for utilities, NHK said.
The moves also come as demand for electricity is expected to rise sharply, driven by data centres for AI.
Under the current energy plan, Japan aims to raise the share of nuclear power in its electricity mix to around 20 per cent by fiscal 2040, up from 9.4 per cent in fiscal 2024.
The draft policy is scheduled to be discussed on Friday before a formal adoption at a ministerial meeting later this summer, NHK said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Canada unveils national AI strategy
Article:
OTTAWA: Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney on Thursday launched the country's new national artificial intelligence (AI) strategy, reported Xinhua.
The strategy, named "AI for All," targets a boost to Canada's economy by nearly C$200 billion (about US$144 billion) to create 250,000 new AI-related jobs over the next five years, said a news release on the prime minister's official website.
It also aims to increase AI adoption from just over 12 per cent to 60 per cent by 2034, said the release, adding that a national AI literacy initiative will also be established to offer entry-level AI training for all Canadians.
Small and medium-sized businesses will get help adopting AI to support workers, raise productivity, and drive breakthroughs in priority sectors, such as health, energy, transportation, agriculture, manufacturing, robotics, and government services, it said.
The strategy will build a world-leading public AI supercomputer and invest in sovereign compute and cloud infrastructure, with a focus on high-performance computing that is sustainable and aligned with Canada's clean energy expansion, robust environmental standards, and tangible benefits for local communities, said the release.
Canada currently has three national AI institutes: the Vector Institute in Toronto, Ontario; Mila - Quebec Artificial Intelligence Institute in Montreal, Quebec; and the Alberta Machine Intelligence Institute in Edmonton, Alberta.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-SB officer avoids jail, fined RM10,000 over bribe from IS suspect's father
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A former Special Branch counter-terrorism officer who accepted a RM10,500 bribe from the father of a man suspected of links to the Islamic State (IS) was fined RM10,000 instead of being jailed after the court considered his wife's pregnancy.
Kuala Terengganu Sessions Court judge Mohd Azhar Othman ordered former assistant superintendent Mohd Nazli Mohamed, 44, to serve nine months in jail if he fails to pay the fine.
Azhar said he did not impose a jail sentence as Mohd Nazli's wife is due to give birth to their third child this month.
Nazli, who was attached to the Terengganu Special Branch Counter-Terrorism Division (E8), was convicted of receiving money without lawful consideration from Zaid Jusoh through five transactions between May and December 2018.
He was charged under Section 165 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum prison sentence of two years, a fine, or both.
According to court documents, Zaid's son, Ahmad Naim, had travelled to Syria and was under police scrutiny over suspected links to IS.
The court heard that Nazli approached Zaid through his official duties as an E8 officer tasked with monitoring and gathering intelligence on Ahmad Naim's whereabouts and activities.
He told Zaid that he could help bring Ahmad Naim and his family home through unofficial channels with the assistance of a man known as Aki Mus.
Believing efforts were being made to reunite him with his son, Zaid handed over money on several occasions, purportedly to cover operational expenses and arrangements for the repatriation mission.
However, the court found no evidence to support the existence of Aki Mus or that Nazli had made any genuine effort to bring the family back to the country.
In his grounds of judgment, Azhar said the accused had exploited a father's desperation to secure the return of his son and family from a conflict zone.
"The accused took advantage of Zaid's desperation to bring home his son and family. He was prepared to do whatever was necessary in the hope that his son could be brought back safely," he said.
The judge said the evidence showed all payments were made because of the accused's position as a counter-terrorism officer and his purported ability to facilitate the family's return.
"There is no evidence that the accused undertook any real effort to bring Naim and his family home. The alleged involvement of Aki Mus was also not supported by any credible evidence before the court," he said.
The judge also rejected the accused's contention that two of the payments, amounting to RM4,500, were personal loans, citing the absence of loan agreements, repayment terms or any supporting evidence.
"The court finds the claim that the payments were loans to be a mere afterthought," he said.
In passing sentence, Azhar said he had considered both aggravating and mitigating factors before deciding that a fine would suffice.
"This fine is imposed on the basis that this is the accused's first offence. He has been placed under interdiction following the charge.
"The accused's wife is due to give birth to their third child at the end of May or early June. Some financial arrangements need to be made for this. On this basis, the court imposes the above fine.
"If not for the fact that the accused's wife is due to give birth, a custodial sentence would have been more appropriate and suitable," he said.
Deputy public prosecutor Farah Yasmin Salleh prosecuted, while lawyer G. Ravishankar represented the accused.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'Pay back or face court': No compromise for deliberate Mara loan defaulters, says chairman
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Majlis Amanah Rakyat (Mara) said that it will not compromise with borrowers who deliberately default on their loans and is prepared to take them to court to safeguard public funds.
Mara chairman Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said funds disbursed are public trust funds, not personal money, making it the borrowers' responsibility to repay them.
"Since I was entrusted with the role of chairman, pursuing defaulting borrowers, including through court action, has been one of my priorities to ensure every sen of public funds is recovered and can be reused to benefit others.
"Mara Council's directive to management is very clear: do not entertain requests for exemptions, even from influential people, regardless of how high their position may be," he said in a Facebook post today, responding to criticism over Mara's decision to bring defaulting borrowers to court.
Asyraf Wajdi said the strict approach was part of the Mara Transformation Programme currently being implemented.
Yesterday FMT reported that a Mara borrower, a 45-year-old woman, and her three guarantors were ordered by the Sungai Petani High Court to repay RM857,000 in outstanding education loans.
Siti Norbaya Ahmad Nor received her first loan in 2011 to pursue a master's degree at the University of Manchester in the UK.
She then secured a second loan in 2013 to do a PhD at the University of Cambridge.
Mara initiated legal proceedings in 2022 after alleging that Siti Norbaya had failed to service the accumulated debt.
Last year, Mara's non-performing loans (NPLs) reached 49.6 per cent, amounting to RM893 million, prompting the agency to consider action, including legal proceedings, against borrowers who failed to repay their loans.
Asyraf said Mara would not compromise or entertain requests from borrowers who attempted to avoid repayment by submitting letters bearing the names of prominent figures, adding that the money belonged to the public.
As of October 2024, a total of 97,866 of Mara's 197,348 active borrowers had been blacklisted.
The blacklist applies to borrowers who fail to service their loans for three months and is enforced solely within Mara's management system without involving other agencies or ecosystems.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mortgage release process is long-standing practice, not new policy
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The Public Sector Housing Financing Board (LPPSA) has clarified that the process of discharging a charge or transferring ownership rights after a housing loan has been fully settled is a long-established practice and not a new policy, as perceived by some parties.
In a statement today, LPPSA said it was aware of the confusion arising from recent announcements on the matter and sought to address misconceptions surrounding the procedure.
"The process of discharging a charge or transferring ownership rights after financing has been fully settled has long been practised as part of the procedures following the completion of financing.
"The responsibility for completing the discharge of charge or transfer of ownership rights has always rested with the borrower," the statement said.
LPPSA advised borrowers to ensure that the process is completed promptly and properly to avoid potential complications in the future, particularly in matters involving inheritance, estate administration and property transfers.
To facilitate the process, LPPSA encourages borrowers to appoint a lawyer to manage the necessary procedures and documentation until completion, ensuring that all requirements are handled in an orderly and efficient manner.
However, it stressed that the recommendation to engage legal services is intended solely to safeguard borrowers' interests and ensure the smooth completion of the discharge process, and should not be viewed as a new obligation or an additional financial burden.
"The recommendation to appoint a lawyer is aimed at protecting borrowers' interests and ensuring that the discharge of charge process is completed properly. It is not intended to create new obligations or impose additional costs on borrowers," the statement said.
LPPSA emphasised that borrowers may still choose to handle the process themselves, provided they comply with all procedures and requirements stipulated by the relevant authorities.
The board noted that its observations found some borrowers had not completed the discharge of charge process despite having fully settled their financing, potentially leading to legal and administrative complications later on.
"LPPSA remains committed to providing transparent, efficient and customer-friendly services, while continuing efforts to raise awareness among borrowers on the importance of completing the discharge of charge or transfer of ownership rights once financing has been fully settled," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Explain restrictions on The Coverage Media's FB page, Teo tells MCMC
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Deputy Communications Minister Teo Nie Ching has called on the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) to provide an immediate explanation over the restriction imposed on The Coverage Media's Facebook page.
Teo said that while she has consistently maintained a policy of non-interference in MCMC's independent enforcement operations, the commission should clarify the reasons behind the action to ensure transparency and public confidence.
"I have always maintained a policy of non-interference regarding MCMC's independent enforcement operations.
"However, I strongly urge MCMC to provide an immediate explanation for the restriction placed on The Coverage Media's Facebook page," she said in a Facebook post.
Earlier, The Coverage Media said its Facebook page had been restricted in Malaysia beginning June 3, marking the first such action taken
against the portal
in more than 10 years of operation.
In a statement, the portal said it had previously reported critically on governments and political leaders across different administrations without facing similar restrictions.
The portal also rejected claims circulating online that it was linked to MCA, saying it had no connection with the party.
The Coverage Media said several allegations had surfaced in the days leading up to the restriction, including claims that it was affiliated with MCA and was attempting to promote support for Barisan Nasional or Perikatan Nasional through articles critical of PKR and DAP.
It said the allegations were untrue and reiterated that it operates independently.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Wall St ends higher; Dow claims record closing high
Article:
NEW YORK: Wall Street advanced on Thursday as progress toward ending the Iran war buoyed investor sentiment, while disappointing results from Broadcom led a chip selloff that held the Nasdaq's gains in check.
The blue-chip Dow surged, hitting a record closing high with a boost from healthcare and financial stocks.
The S&P 500 posted more muted gains, while the Nasdaq ended essentially unchanged. Chipmaker Broadcom missed revenue expectations, sending its shares tumbling and casting a pall over the AI frenzy, which has sent chip stocks soaring so far this year.
"About the only blemish on the market at this point is Broadcom, and I think investors are buying the dip," said Paul Nolte, senior wealth adviser and market strategist at Murphy & Sylvest in Elmhurst, Illinois. "I don't think investors have given up on chips yet, but what they've yet to come to grips with, 'Is this real? Are these valuations legitimate?' I'm not sure yet that investors have really questioned that." The US House of Representatives passed a measure on Wednesday that would block President Donald Trump from continuing the war on Iran. Additionally, a US-mediated ceasefire agreement between Israel and Lebanon, an essential condition of an Iranian agreement to a peace deal, bolstered optimism of a near-term resolution to the war. But the truce was rejected by the pro-Iran Hezbollah, which said it would not withdraw troops from Lebanon.
A drop in front-month crude futures reflected hopes that tanker traffic through the crucial Strait of Hormuz could shortly resume.
"How many deals have we had? It's always right around the corner, a corner we've yet to reach," Nolte added. "Things are moving, but are they moving at a pace that's going to allow the world to get back to what passes for normal in a few weeks, a few months, or maybe sometime next year?"
On the economic front, initial jobless claims unexpectedly rose 6.1 per cent, and first-quarter labor costs and productivity were revised sharply lower. A report from Challenger, Gray and Christmas showed layoffs announced by US corporations jumped 11 per cent in May to 97,006. Nearly 40 per cent of those layoffs were attributed to AI.
According to preliminary data, the S&P 500 gained 31.14 points, or 0.41 per cent, to end at 7,584.82 points, while the Nasdaq Composite lost 19.72 points, or 0.07 per cent, to 26,834.26. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 875.09 points, or 1.73 per cent, to 51,562.16.
Chipmaker Marvell Technology gained, while Advanced Micro Devices, Micron Technology and Qualcomm lost ground on the day.
The healthcare sector got a boost from UnitedHealth after Bank of America raised its rating on the healthcare conglomerate's shares to "buy."
The financial index's rebound followed a sharp selloff in the previous session due to revived concerns over private credit. Blackstone shares advanced after it became the latest asset manager to cap withdrawals from its flagship private credit fund following a rise in redemption requests.
Cybersecurity firm CrowdStrike slumped after reporting an increase in quarterly operating expenses.
An investor roadshow for Elon Musk-led SpaceX began on Thursday ahead of its market debut on June 12. It aims to raise US$75 billion in a record IPO that would value it at US$1.75 trillion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PH to launch Johor election machinery tomorrow
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Pakatan Harapan (PH) will launch its election machinery for the Johor polls in Batu Pahat tomorrow night.
PH chairman Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim is expected to officiate the launch at Dewan Sekolah Tinggi Cina alongside Amanah president Datuk Seri Mohamad Sabu and DAP secretary-general Anthony Loke.
In a Facebook post, Batu Pahat PKR called on the public to attend the event.
"Johor people are ready. Johor is not afraid to face an election. Johor will not remain silent when the future of the state is at stake. Johor is ready to determine the direction of this state.
"Fill the hall. Rock Batu Pahat. Send a message that Johor people are ready," it said.
The Johor state assembly was dissolved on June 1, paving the way for an election that must be held within 60 days.
In the 2022 state election, Barisan Nasional secured a two-thirds majority after winning 40 of the 56 seats contested. Pakatan Harapan won 12 seats, while Perikatan Nasional and Muda secured three and one seat, respectively.
Three-way contests are expected throughout Johor with BN, PH, and PN set to contest all 56 seats.
https://www.nst.com.my/news/regional/2026/06/1453006/johor-assembly-dissolved-election-within-60-days-watch
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US imposes sanctions on Cuban president, Castro family members
Article:
HAVANA: The United States announced new sanctions Thursday on Cuba's president and some of his immediate family, alongside members of the Castro family, in Washington's latest ramping up of pressure on its communist-led neighbor.
Among those targeted were the son and a grandson of former president Raul Castro, who no longer holds an official position but remains a key power-broker on the island.
President Miguel Diaz-Canel, his wife and stepson were also hit by the latest flurry of US sanctions, as was the Ministry of the Revolutionary Armed Forces and several other entities.
While Cuba has been under a US trade embargo since 1962, President Donald Trump has drastically ramped up pressure in recent months, cutting off its fuel supplies and threatening to take over the island.
Washington claims that Cuba's communist regime threatens the security of Americans.
Trump has repeatedly stated it could be next to fall, after the January overthrow of Venezuela's socialist leader Nicolas Maduro and the US campaign against Iran.
"We'll take care of the Islamic Republic of Iran, and as soon as that's done, on our way back, we'll just make a little brief stop over," Trump said Thursday, alluding to Cuba.
He denied that the new sanctions, which follow a US murder indictment against Raul Castro and sanctions on a military conglomerate that controls much of Cuba's economy, were aimed at accelerating the island's collapse.
Washington had already taken aim at Diaz-Canel last year, banning him and his family from entering the United States.
Writing on X, Diaz-Canel accused Trump of trying to "strengthen the blockade and scenario of conflict between Cuba and the United States."
He added that Cuba would resist the "aggressiveness and perversity of the Yankee government" and "the imperialist onslaught."
Foreign Minister Bruno Rodriguez said the "vile" sanctions would be met with "greater unity and determination from our people."
Trump told reporters at the White House he just wanted Cuba to be "a nicely run country that can feed its people.
"But the country is starving, and it's got no energy, it's got no oil, it's got no money, it's got nothing."
He noted however that Cuba has "a beautiful piece of land," musing: "You could have beautiful resorts."
- 'Explosive cocktail' -
The fuel embargo in place since January has left Cuba without diesel for generators used to bolster its crumbling power supply, resulting in power outages of up to 22 hours a day and shortages of tap water.
With transport virtually at a standstill, the island is also running short on food and medicine, making it reliant on aid shipments from Mexico and China.
The United Nations representative on the island warned Thursday that the humanitarian emergency, coupled with the onset of the Caribbean hurricane season, made for an "explosive cocktail."
The east of the island is still recovering from Hurricane Melissa, which caused major destruction in October last year. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DAP to defend all seats in Johor and Negri, postpones special congress
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: DAP will defend all its seats in Johor and Negri Sembilan and is seeking to contest additional seats in Johor in the upcoming state elections, says secretary-general Anthony Loke.
Loke also announced that the DAP Central Executive Committee (CEC) had decided to postpone the party's special congress from July 12 to Aug 16.
It was previously reported that the special congress would allow delegates to decide whether DAP office-bearers should relinquish all positions held in the government.
In a statement last night, he said the final allocation of seats would depend on negotiations with Pakatan Harapan component parties, which are expected to conclude by this weekend.
He said the party's candidate selection committee for the state elections would comprise himself, DAP chairman Gobind Singh Deo, deputy chairman Nga Kor Ming, DAP Wanita chief Teo Nie Ching and parliamentary leader Chong Chieng Jen.
Loke also said DAP supported the tabling of a bill to separate the roles of the Attorney-General and Public Prosecutor during the Dewan Rakyat sitting beginning on June 22.
"DAP also supports the tabling of the bill for the separation of the roles of the Attorney-General and Public Prosecutor, which has been finalised by the Parliamentary Special Select Committee, in the Dewan Rakyat sitting that convenes on June 22.
"DAP is committed to ensuring block vote support from all 40 DAP members of parliament and urges all MPs to ensure this historic constitutional amendment can be realised," he said.
Loke said DAP remained committed to advancing the government's institutional reform agenda and would campaign vigorously in the
Johor
and Negri Sembilan elections to help secure victories for Pakatan Harapan in both states.
The Johor state assembly was dissolved on June 1, while the
Negri Sembilan state assembly
was dissolved today. Elections must be held within 60 days of a state assembly's dissolution.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Correcting without criticising
Article:
LEADERSHIP involves more than setting direction, motivating people, and celebrating success.
It also requires addressing poor performance, inappropriate behaviour, and mistakes when they occur.
Many leaders find reprimanding staff uncomfortable because they worry about damaging relationships, lowering morale, or creating conflict.
However, avoiding difficult conversations can allow problems to grow and affect the wider team. Effective leaders understand that reprimanding staff is sometimes necessary, but it must be done professionally and without becoming personal.
The purpose of a reprimand is to correct behaviour, not to punish the individual.
When leaders lose sight of this distinction, conversations can quickly become emotional and counterproductive. Staff members are more likely to accept feedback when they believe the leader is focused on improving performance rather than attacking their character.
The objective should always be to help the individual understand the issue and take steps towards improvement.
A common mistake made by inexperienced leaders is linking a person's actions to their identity.
Statements such as "you are careless" or "you are unreliable" can feel like personal attacks. Instead, leaders should focus on observable behaviours and specific outcomes.
Saying "the report was submitted late" or "the agreed process was not followed" keeps the discussion centred on facts. This approach reduces defensiveness and encourages a more constructive conversation.
Preparation is an important part of any reprimand. Leaders should gather relevant information and ensure they understand the situation before speaking to the employee.
Acting on assumptions or incomplete information can undermine credibility and create resentment. Taking time to review the facts allows the leader to address the matter fairly and confidently.
Timing also matters. Difficult conversations should generally take place as soon as reasonably possible after the issue occurs.
Delaying feedback can make it harder for the employee to connect the discussion with the behaviour in question. At the same time, leaders should avoid addressing issues while angry or frustrated.
Strong emotions can lead to comments that feel personal and damage trust.
Privacy is another key consideration. Reprimanding staff in front of colleagues can cause embarrassment and humiliation.
Even when a mistake is obvious to others, corrective conversations should usually be conducted in private. Public criticism often creates fear and resentment, whereas private discussions allow people to listen, respond, and reflect with dignity intact.
During the conversation, leaders should communicate clearly and directly. Vague feedback can create confusion and uncertainty.
Employees need to understand exactly what happened, why it is a concern, and what is expected going forward. Clear communication reduces misunderstandings and helps establish accountability.
However, clarity does not require harshness. Firmness and respect can coexist.
Listening is equally important. Effective reprimands are not one-sided lectures. Leaders should give employees an opportunity to explain their perspective.
There may be circumstances or challenges that were not initially apparent. Listening does not mean excusing poor performance, but it demonstrates fairness and respect.
Employees are more likely to engage positively when they feel heard.
A professional leader separates intent from impact. Sometimes employees make mistakes despite having good intentions.
While intentions may provide useful context, leaders must still address the consequences of the behaviour.
Focusing on impact allows the discussion to remain objective. The conversation becomes about what occurred and how it affected the team, rather than speculation about motives or character.
Language plays a significant role in maintaining professionalism. Leaders should avoid sarcasm, insults, ridicule, or emotionally charged comments.  Such language rarely improves performance and often damages relationships. Calm, measured communication sends a clear message while preserving respect.
Staff may not enjoy receiving criticism, but they are more likely to accept it when delivered professionally.
Consistency is another essential leadership quality. Employees quickly notice when standards are applied unevenly.
If one person is reprimanded for a particular issue while another is ignored for similar behaviour, perceptions of favouritism can emerge.
Consistent application of expectations reinforces fairness and strengthens trust in leadership. It also helps ensure that reprimands are viewed as legitimate rather than personal.
Leaders should also focus on future improvement rather than dwelling excessively on past mistakes.
Once the issue has been discussed and understood, attention should shift towards solutions and expectations. Employees need to know what success looks like and how they can achieve it.
Constructive guidance provides a pathway forward and increases the likelihood of positive change.
Following up after a reprimand is equally important. Improvement rarely happens through a single conversation. Leaders should monitor progress, provide support where appropriate, and acknowledge positive changes.
Recognition of improvement reinforces desired behaviours and demonstrates that the purpose of the reprimand was development rather than punishment.
Maintaining respect throughout the process is fundamental. Every employee deserves to be treated with dignity, regardless of the seriousness of the issue being addressed.
Respectful treatment preserves relationships and supports a positive workplace culture. It also reflects emotional maturity and professionalism on the part of the leader.
Ultimately, reprimanding staff without making it personal is a skill that every leader must develop. It requires preparation, emotional control, clear communication, fairness, and respect.
When handled effectively, corrective conversations can strengthen accountability, improve performance, and reinforce professional standards.
By focusing on behaviour rather than personality, leaders can address problems directly while maintaining trust and preserving the dignity of the individuals involved.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Corporate earnings outlook turns cautious, weak quarter raises red flags [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia's corporate earnings outlook has turned more challenging after companies delivered a softer set of results for the latest first quarter (Q1) of 2026.
The performance reflects seasonally weaker performance and growing caution amid rising Middle East crisis, analysts said.
RHB Research said four key sectors - banks, plantations, property and rubber products - missed expectations during the quarter, with no earnings beats recorded.
This marked a sharp reversal from the previous quarter, which saw multiple upside surprises.
Only 12.1 per cent of companies under the firm's portfolio reported earnings above forecasts, the lowest proportion since September 2018.
Meanwhile, 31.8 per cent of firms missed estimates, pushing the miss-to-beat ratio to 2.6 times from 1.6 times in the December 2025 quarter.
The weaker earnings trend was reflected in several major corporate results.
In banking, Malayan Banking Bhd's first-quarter net profit fell 4.2 per cent to RM2.48 billion, while CIMB Group Holdings Bhd posted a slight decline in net profit to RM1.92 billion.
Plantation heavyweight SD Guthrie Bhd also recorded a marginal earnings decline as weaker upstream performance weighed on results.
IJM Corp Bhd slipped into a net loss of RM173.89 million for its fourth quarter ended March 2026, mainly due to foreign exchange losses.
In the rubber products space, Kossan Rubber Industries Bhd reported higher net profit, although the improvement was largely supported by lower tax expenses despite weaker revenue.
Earnings Revisions Mixed As Recovery Remains Uneven
RHB Research said normalised earnings revisions for the broader market turned negative for financial years 2026 and 2027 (FY26 and FY27), falling 1.3 per cent and 1.1 per cent respectively.
This was after excluding Petronas Chemicals Group Bhd, which benefited from improved product spreads following higher oil prices.
On a nominal basis, earnings revisions increased slightly by 1.2 per cent for FY26 but declined 0.8 per cent for FY27, highlighting uneven sector recovery.
Upward revisions were seen in oil and gas, real estate investment trusts, non-bank financial institutions, telecommunications and property.
These gains were offset by downward revisions in banks, plantations, technology and energy.
For FBM KLCI large-cap stocks, FY26 earnings revisions were slightly positive at 2 per cent, largely supported by Petronas Chemicals.
Excluding the group, aggregate earnings estimates were cut by 1.5 per cent for FY26 and 1.3 per cent for FY27.
RHB Research estimated downside support for the FBM KLCI at around 1,433 points and trimmed its end-2026 target to 1,750 points from 1,780 points, citing unchanged valuation multiples on FY27 earnings.
CIMB Securities also maintained a cautious stance on the Q1 earnings season, noting softer-than-expected results across its coverage universe.
Of the 97 companies it tracks, 12 per cent beat expectations, down from 26 per cent in Q4 of 2025, while 24 per cent missed, up from 19 per cent previously.
This translates into a lower beat-to-miss ratio of 0.52 times versus 1.33 times in the prior quarter.
It said underperformance was concentrated in construction, property and consumer sectors, which accounted for more than half of the weaker-than-expected results amid volatile conditions, higher costs and softer sales linked to geopolitical tensions.
In contrast, Kenanga Research said the Q1 results provided a solid earnings buffer for the rest of the year, with a high proportion of companies meeting or exceeding expectations across both the FBM KLCI and broader coverage universe.
"This provides a strong buffer to earnings, as many companies have also sought to lock in inventory or pass on costs," it said.
Kenanga Research noted that some exuberance in the market has moderated, with technology and oil and gas sectors seeing the most downgrades following the results season.
It said peak oil-price-driven optimism is now behind the oil and gas sector and lowered its outlook on Petronas Chemicals, while maintaining that artificial intelligence and front-end technology exposures still retain a strong growth runway.
The firm continues to see selective buying opportunities where concerns have become excessive, noting that the ratio of downgrades to upgrades remained stable quarter-on-quarter, with upgrades seen in consumer-related stocks.
It highlighted opportunities in names such as Petronas Dagangan Bhd, QL Resources Bhd and Padini Holdings Bhd, where valuations have fallen to multi-year lows or already priced in inflation and subsidy removal risks.
Kenanga Research also flagged sector rotation within its stock picks, remaining positive on plantations but cautious on Indonesia policy risks related to centralised procurement, favouring IOI Corp Bhd as a more insulated play.
Within banks, it raised Public Bank Bhd as its top sector pick, replacing CIMB, noting that earlier caution on the sector had played out well and risks are now more contained, particularly for larger banks.
Kenanga Research said Malaysian corporates remain well prepared and it has turned slightly more constructive, lifting its end-2026 FBM KLCI target to 1,770 points from 1,760 points.
It added that earnings growth of about eight per cent is flattered by Petronas Chemicals, but excluding the group, FBM KLCI earnings growth would still remain a respectable 4.8 per cent for 2026.
Following dividend surprises in fourth-quarter 2025, value-unlocking initiatives continued in the current quarter, including from Public Bank and IJM Corp Bhd, while Telekom Malaysia Bhd proceeded with a higher payout ratio as guided.
SP Setia Bhd and Frontken Group Bhd have been included in its latest stock picks.
6ScGB6mgktE
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elon Musk 'trying to whip up division' over UK student's murder: PM
Article:
LONDON: UK leader Keir Starmer accused US tech tycoon Elon Musk on Thursday of "trying to whip up division" in Britain following anger over the police handling of the murder of a white student by a Sikh man.
The case of 18-year-old Henry Nowak, who was handcuffed by police as he lay dying after being stabbed by Vickrum Digwa in the southern city of Southampton in December, has become highly politicised in the UK.
Digwa, 23, lied and told police he was the victim and that Nowak had racially insulted him.
The UK prime minister met Nowak's mother, father and stepmother at Downing Street late Thursday for about an hour.
Afterwards, Starmer said the country must "choose unity and progress over division and hatred", calling this "the only way to honour Henry's memory."
Far-right figures have claimed the murder is evidence that police forces in Britain treat white people and ethnic minorities differently – an allegation Starmer's Labour government and police chiefs vehemently deny.
Musk, the billionaire owner of X, has posted numerous times on the platform about the police response to the stabbing.
In one, he falsely asked whether people knew that "official police policy requires them to be racist against Whites?"
Black people in England and Wales are more than twice as likely to be arrested as white people, according to government statistics.
And an independent report last year found Britain's largest police force, London's Metropolitan Police, was "institutionally racist."
Musk has offered to fund a private prosecution against the police over its handling of the murder and insulted the Hampshire Police force involved.
"We need to also assert who we are as a country, because Musk, again, has been interfering in our politics in the last few days, trying to whip up division," Starmer told reporters.
"When we have a terrible case like Henry's case ... we react calmly, as his family have done," Starmer said, following pleas from Nowak's father that his son's murder should not be used "to create further division, hatred or tension."
Digwa was jailed for at least 21 years on Monday for stabbing Nowak to death using a knife with a 21-centimetre (eight-inch) blade following an altercation about a mobile phone.
Starmer has said there was "no justification" for violence at a Southampton protest on Tuesday night attended by far-right agitators, which saw demonstrators throw bricks, flares and chairs at police officers.
A 44-year-old man on Thursday pleaded guilty to violent disorder and carrying an offensive weapon. Another person has been charged with assault.
[[nid:1455668]]
The prime minister said it was "unforgivable" that hard-right firebrand Nigel Farage, whose Reform UK party is leading in the polls, had called for people to respond to the murder with "pure cold rage."
Farage, who has been accused of stoking racial tensions with his remarks, has defended his comments.
"Of course I condemn all violence ... I've never, in 35 years of being in politics, advocated people going outside the law," he told the GB News television channel.
The US State Department has also waded into the debate, offering condolences to Nowak's family, and adding: "Ideological conditioning and two-tiered policing are glaring symptoms of civilisational decline. They must be rejected across the West."
Starmer has called bodycam footage of Nowak's death, during which the victim can repeatedly be heard telling officers he could not breathe, "harrowing."
On Thursday, he said there were "difficult questions that need to be answered about the way the police handled Henry's murder."
The Independent Office for Police Conduct (IOPC) watchdog is investigating.
An inquest into whether police contributed to Nowak's death will open in front of a jury in September 2027, officials announced Thursday.
Musk has long been an outspoken critic of Starmer, who previously served as a chief state prosecutor.
In a clash last year over a decades-long "grooming gangs" sexual abuse scandal, Starmer accused the world's richest man of "spreading lies and misinformation." - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pas eyes broader PN+ coalition with Umno among potential partners [WATCH]
Article:
KOTA BARU: Pas is looking into the possibility of reactivating the Perikatan Nasional Plus (PN+) formula, an expanded coalition involving existing component parties as well as new political groups aligned with the opposition bloc.
Pas deputy president Datuk Seri Tuan Ibrahim Tuan Man said the proposal was among the recommendations made during the party's recent syura council meeting.
"At the syura council meeting on Sunday, we discussed our ties with Bersatu.
"Among the recommendations put forward by council members was the formation of PN+.
"Pas is now focusing on establishing this. If the initiative does not materialise, we will consider other options. For now, we will work towards making it a success," said the Kubang Kerian member of parliament.
Tuan Ibrahim, who is also PN deputy chairman, said the proposal was among several suggestions aimed at strengthening and improving the coalition.
It is understood that the syura council recommended that the "Reset" movement led by former Bersatu deputy president Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin be a part of PN+.
"Among parties being considered (for PN+) are Pejuang, Berjasa and others, including Umno."
Tuan Ibrahim said PN+ would include parties seeking to join the coalition.
"For Pas, those who were expelled from Bersatu are free to join PN as they continue to support the coalition."
Asked if Pas would consider forging its own path outside PN if efforts to establish PN+ failed, Tuan Ibrahim said the matter would be examined in greater detail at a later stage.
PN comprises Pas, Bersatu, Gerakan and the Malaysian Indian People's Party.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump foe Bolton to plead guilty to retaining classified info
Article:
WASHINGTON: John Bolton, who served as Donald Trump's national security advisor before becoming an outspoken critic of the US president, plans to plead guilty to one count of retaining classified documents, US media reported on Thursday.
The 77-year-old veteran diplomat is expected to enter the guilty plea at a hearing in federal district court in Maryland on June 26, CNN and other outlets said.
Bolton, one of several Trump foes to be hit with criminal charges since the Republican president returned to the White House, was indicted in October on 18 counts of transmitting and retaining top secret national defence information.
CNN said Bolton will plead guilty to one felony count of illegal retention of sensitive national security information, which carries a sentence of up to 60 months in prison. He will also pay a fine of more than US$2 million.
The plea agreement will need to be approved by a judge.
Bolton was accused of sharing classified files by email with two "unauthorized individuals" who have not been identified but are believed to be his wife and daughter.
The material was allegedly later used in a book that Bolton wrote, "The Room Where It Happened," that was highly critical of the Trump administration.
Bolton, who served as Trump's national security advisor during his first term, frequently appears on television news shows and in print to condemn the man he has called "unfit to be president."
Trump has repeatedly lashed out at his former aide and withdrew his security detail shortly after returning to the White House in January of last year.
The Trump Justice Department has sought to bring cases against other prominent critics of the president, including New York Attorney General Letitia James and former FBI director James Comey.
Comey was indicted in April for allegedly threatening Trump's life, five months after a previous case against the ex-FBI chief was thrown out.
The indictment by a grand jury in North Carolina stems from an Instagram post Comey made that showed the numbers "86 47" spelled out in seashells on a beach.
Trump has alleged that "86" was slang for kill and "47" was a reference to his being the 47th president.
Unlike the James and Comey cases, however, the investigation into Bolton's alleged mishandling of classified material began during the administration of former president Joe Biden. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia must strengthen trade checks to counter US tariff threat [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:    Malaysia must strengthen enforcement and supply chain checks amid  scrutiny from the United States over forced labour and excess capacity concerns, said legal experts.
The country's existing laws are sufficient but clearer proof is needed that trade channels are not used to bypass international rules, they added.
Their comments follow remarks by Investment, Trade and Industry Minister Datuk Seri Johari Abdul Ghani that Malaysia has limited powers over goods sourced from third countries.
The issue has come under renewed scrutiny following a review by the US Trade Representative (USTR), which cited concerns over forced labour and alleged excess capacity as grounds for a proposed 10 per cent tariff on certain Malaysian exports.
LACK OF VERIFICATION
Senior advocate and international commercial arbitrator GK Ganesan Kasinathan said it would be inaccurate to suggest that Malaysia lacks the legal authority to act.
Instead, he  argued that the real challenge lies in verifying the origin of products and detecting attempts to circumvent trade rules.
"With respect, the powers are not limited. What is limited is our capacity to verify what passes through our own ports," he told the
New Straits Times
.
Ganesan noted that since May 6 last year, the ministry has become the sole issuer of non-preferential certificates of origin for exports destined for the US, a move designed to strengthen oversight and reduce the risk of transshipment abuses.
He also noted existing provisions under the Customs Act 1967, which criminalises false declarations relating to the origin or value of goods, as well as the Strategic Trade Act 2010, which grants authorities powers over transit and transshipment activities.
According to Ganesan, the problem is operational rather than legislative.
He said transshipment fraud often involves products being routed through intermediary countries before reaching their final destination, making it difficult to determine their true origin.
Ganesan added that certificates of origin alone were insufficient if they were not backed by robust audits and verification mechanisms.
STRONGER ENFORCEMENT NEEDED
Ganesan said Malaysia should use the period before any final US tariff decision to demonstrate its commitment to enforcement.
Among the measures he proposed are publishing records of audits and enforcement actions, strengthening supply chain verification procedures and improving transparency around origin certification.
Universiti Teknologi Malaysia associate professor in property economics Dr Muhammad Najib Razali said Malaysia already has laws to act against fake origin claims, false export documents and Customs fraud.
However, he  noted  that Malaysia still needs stronger enforcement and clearer proof systems because the current US concern was not only whether Malaysia has laws, but whether it could prove that goods exported were genuinely made or substantially processed in Malaysia.
"The real issue is not whether Malaysia has no law. The issue is whether Malaysia's enforcement is strong enough to convince the US and other trading partners. In trade disputes, confidence matters.
"Malaysia must show that it can detect suspicious shipments early, check whether exports are genuinely Malaysian and punish companies that misuse the system," he said.
NEED FOR A RULES-BASED TRADING FRAMEWORK
Nizam Bashir & Associates managing partner Nizam Bashir agreed that Malaysia already has sufficient legal authority but said enforcement mechanisms must evolve to address increasingly complex trade risks.
He said concerns surrounding forced labour-linked imports and supply-chain circumvention have become more prominent as global trade authorities intensify scrutiny of product origins.
"The statutory tools are there, but enforcement has not kept pace with the scale of circumvention risk," he said.
Nizam outlined six immediate priorities for policymakers.
The first is ensuring Malaysia submits a comprehensive response to the USTR consultation process before the deadline.
Secondly, he said the government should consider introducing a specific prohibition on imports linked to forced labour.
Other measures include strengthening origin verification procedures, publishing enforcement statistics, establishing a trusted-exporter regime and formalising cooperation among relevant government agencies.
"That combination gives Malaysia its strongest legal answer. We may have at some point merely been a conduit economy; we are moving towards a rules-based trading jurisdiction with enforceable origin, labour and Customs controls," he said.
CREDIBILITY, NOT JUST COMPLIANCE
Economist Samirul Ariff Othman said Malaysia's challenge was no longer about whether laws exist, but whether authorities could convincingly demonstrate that those laws were being enforced.
He noted that Malaysia already has several key legal instruments, including the Customs Act 1967, the Strategic Trade Act 2010, labour regulations and the country's certificate-of-origin framework.
"Malaysia has enough basic laws to act, but not enough to assume immunity from US suspicion," he said.
"The weak point is not formal legality. It is traceability, audit capacity, beneficial ownership checks, free-zone monitoring, supply-chain due diligence and proving that 'Made in Malaysia' means genuine Malaysian origin, not cosmetic relabelling."
TARIFF PROPOSAL NOT FINAL
Najib said the US tariff  decision was not final, giving  Malaysia an  opportunity to respond before a final decision is made.
He noted  the most likely near-term outcome was continued negotiation. Malaysia will likely keep engaging with the US to prevent the tariff from becoming final, reduce its scope or get exemptions for compliant sectors.
"However, if Malaysia cannot provide convincing evidence, the risk remains that the US may impose the tariff or keep Malaysia under closer review," he said.
Moving forward, Najib said the best approach was not to ban third-country trade broadly.
"Malaysia should instead strengthen audits, origin verification, Customs cooperation, supply-chain checks and forced-labour controls. This will protect genuine Malaysian exporters, reassure investors and reduce the risk that Malaysia is treated as a tariff-circumvention hub."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Queen visits Chow Kit school for underprivileged children
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
HER Majesty Raja Zarith Sofiah, the Queen of Malaysia, visited Sekolah Bimbingan Jalinan Kasih for underprivileged children yesterday as part celebrations marking the king's birthday.
According to a post on the Sultan Ibrahim Sultan Iskandar Facebook page, the queen was received by the prime minister's wife Datuk Seri Dr Wan Azizah Wan Ismail, Education Minister Fadhlina Sidek and the Prime Minister's Department senior deputy secretary-general Datuk Abd Shukor Mahmood.
The queen was briefed on the Education Ministry's role, as well as the school's operations and achievements.
The ministry established the school in 2013 with the aspiration of providing education for all.
Sekolah Bimbingan Jalinan Kasih currently provides formal education to 158 marginalised and orphaned children in the Chow Kit area.
Raja Zarith Sofiah also presented a RM30,000 contribution to the school and spent time interacting with its pupils and teachers.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hundreds in Libya protest against migrants, refugees
Article:
TRIPOLI: Hundreds of Libyans gathered outside the UN refugee agency headquarters in Tripoli on Thursday to protest against irregular migrants and refugees whom they say should leave Libya.
Demonstrators chanted "Libya belongs to Libyans" among other chants and called for the closure of the UNHCR headquarters in the capital.
They accused the UN agency of seeking to settle irregular migrants in the North African country.
Protesters were seen holding signs reading: "Our love for our country is not racism" and "Libya is not the world's garbage bin."
Libya is a key departure point for irregular migrants seeking to reach Europe by crossing the Mediterranean.
As of mid-2024, the International Organization for Migration estimated that around 900,000 migrants and refugees were living in Libya.
Many of them are Sudanese refugees who have fled war in their home country.
The UN mission in Libya in a statement said it "refutes allegations of migrant resettlement to Libya."
"The UN in Libya reiterates that none of its agencies, including UNHCR, implement any resettlement programmes to Libya," it said.
The mission said it was "concerned about the spread of misinformation, disinformation and hate speech regarding the work of the UN" in the country. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Undocumented siblings get proper education chance
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
THE Education Ministry and National Registration Department (NRD) are leading an inter-agency effort to help six undocumented siblings who have never attended school.
The intervention follows Monday's report by 'Harian Metro' on the family's situation.
The siblings are being raised by their Indonesian mother. Their Malaysian father died a year ago from an undiagnosed illness.
On Wednesday, officials from the ministry, NRD, Selangor Zakat Board and Hulu Langat District Education Office, as well as representatives from the Hulu Langat parliamentary office, visited the family's home to assess their immediate welfare and educational needs.
NRD officers reviewed the documentation status of the mother and her six children, aged between 6 and 16, as the lack of proper identification was believed to be the main reason they were unable to attend school.
Officials from the ministry and the district education office also reviewed their citizenship status to expedite their enrolment.
The Selangor Zakat Board, in its third visit to the family, sought updates from NRD to facilitate financial and welfare assistance.
The eldest sibling, 16, said the sudden outpouring of support had given his family newfound hope after years of hardship.
"If possible, we would like to go to school. I am happy that there are people willing to help sort out our documents. I hope that everything goes well. Thank you, everyone."
He said his only experience with formal education was attending kindergarten for three months before his family withdrew him due to financial constraints.
The teenager said being undocumented left him in a constant state of anxiety and made it difficult for him to secure a permanent job.
He currently works part-time at a motorcycle workshop, while two younger siblings are selling kuih to help cover the family's expenses.
"With proper documents, I can look for work more confidently and help my mother support the family," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: TNG Digital non-payments business surpasses payments revenue
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
KUALA LUMPUR: TNG Digital Sdn Bhd's non-payments business has overtaken the payments segment in revenue.
TNG Digital said this underscores its transformation from a digital payments provider into a broader financial and lifestyle ecosystem.
Chief executive officer Alan Ni said the non-payments business accounted for 51 per cent of total revenue in 2025.
This was a significant change from 2021, when the payments segment accounted for 75 per cent of total revenue and the company's business was largely associated with toll, parking and payments services.
"We started from mobility, toll and parking. But today, that business accounts for less than three per cent of our total revenue," he said at a media briefing.
"Just four months into 2026, the non-payments business increased to 53 per cent of revenue."
He said the figures highlight a gap between public perception of TNG eWallet and its business model.
The platform now has more than 26 million verified users, including over 23 million Malaysians and nearly three million foreign residents and tourists.
More than 13 million people use the app every month.
Ni said the growth in engagement is driven by services beyond payments.
He said cross-border payments, remittance and international services now account for 10 per cent of total revenue, compared with virtually none in 2021.
Meanwhile, TNG Digital's business-to-business segment, which includes advertising, digital infrastructure and merchant value-added services, accounted for six per cent of total revenue in 2025, and has exceeded 10 per cent on a year-to-date basis in 2026.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: WHO: Unsafe food kills 1.5m people worldwide yearly
Article:
Your browser does not support the audio element.
FOOD contaminated with harmful bacteria, viruses, parasites or chemicals kills 1.5 million people worldwide each year, with young children worst hit, the World Health Organisation (WHO) warned yesterday.
After analysing 194 countries between 2000 and 2021, the United Nations health agency found that 886 million people contract an illness linked to the consumption of unsafe food a year, with children under 5 years old nearly three times more likely to be at risk.
"Food safety is not an abstract issue — it touches every meal, every family, every day," said WHO chief Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus.
Although illnesses from ingesting dangerous food have declined overall since 2000, massive regional inequalities remain.
Africa and Southeast Asia alone account for nearly three-quarters of all cases of food-related ailments and 60 per cent of deaths worldwide.
At some 860 million cases in 2021, biological hazards such as bacteria and viruses caused the overwhelming majority of food-borne illnesses.
But ingesting chemicals was responsible for a disproportionate share of the deaths, with arsenic and lead poisoning the prime non-biological culprits.
"The data show that food-borne diseases are not only persistent but also are being made worse by climate change — which increases contamination risks — and by antimicrobial resistance, which makes infections harder to treat," said Yuki Minato, WHO technical officer for food safety.
Besides the health effects, the study also estimates that food-borne diseases cost the world economy US$647 billion in lost productivity in 2021. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Implementation date for 10-year passport to be announced soon by govt: Immigration DG
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  The Immigration Department is ready to implement changes relating to passport validity of up to 10 years, as provided under the Fees (Passports and Visas) (Amendment) Order 2026 published in the Government Gazette.
Immigration director‑general Datuk Zakaria Shaaban said the implementation date would be announced by the government soon.
"The implementation date will be announced by the government and we are ready to implement it," he said when contacted by Bernama yesterday.
Zakaria said the department is currently issuing passports with a five‑year validity period and would continue to do so, with the public to be given a choice once the new validity period is implemented.
The Fees (Passports and Visas) (Amendment) Order 2026 stipulates that the new Malaysian passport with a validity period of up to 10 years will be issued at RM350, with implementation scheduled for this year.
Individuals aged 60 and above will be charged RM175, while disabled persons aged 18 and above registered under the Persons with Disabilities Act 2008 are entitled to a free passport.
The Government Gazette signed by Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar also sets the fee for the first‑time replacement of damaged or lost passports for citizens aged 13 to 59 at RM550.
For citizens aged over 60, the fee for the first replacement is RM375, RM675 for the second, and RM1,175 for subsequent replacements.
Meanwhile, disabled persons aged 18 and above and the department's personnel are charged RM200 for the first replacement, RM500 for the second, and RM1,000 for subsequent cases.
Currently, Malaysians are charged RM200 for passports with a five‑year validity period, while senior citizens pay RM100.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Restaurant worker under probe over alleged cat abuse in viral video
Article:
IPOH: Police have opened an investigation paper into a case involving a man who allegedly abused a cat at a restaurant in Taman Melor, Teluk Intan.
A 1‑minute 32‑second video showing the man, believed to be a restaurant worker, placing a cat into a sack recently went viral on social media.
Hilir Perak district police chief ACP Chua Kok Lian said following the circulation of the video, police conducted an initial investigation to identify the individual involved, the location of the incident, and to obtain complete details of the case.
"Police have also received a report lodged by the restaurant owner regarding the viral video. Based on the report received, the restaurant worker is believed to be the individual who placed the cat into the sack as shown in the viral video," he said in a statement last night.
Chua said the case is being investigated under Section 29 of the Animal Welfare Act 2015 for suspected animal cruelty.
He added that police will work with the Veterinary Services Department and other relevant agencies to complete the investigation and take appropriate action under existing laws.
"The public is urged to remain mindful of animal welfare and promptly report any incident of animal abuse or neglect to the authorities. Police take all forms of animal cruelty seriously and will not compromise with any party involved in such acts," he said.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tiny Montenegro vies to be EU's next member but hurdles remain
Article:
WHEN Air Montenegro's passenger planes took to the skies earlier this year, they were branded with a new slogan: "28 by 28 - The Next EU Member."
The message sums up the tiny Balkan nation's political ambition to become the 28th member of the European Union in 2028, and the first to join since Croatia in 2013.
Montenegro, with a population of just 630,000, would be easy to absorb into the EU, a bloc of 450 million people, and boost Brussels' stuttering enlargement project ahead of much trickier candidates such as Ukraine.
However, a swift accession is by no means certain and 2028 is a highly ambitious target.
The former Yugoslav republic must still pass tough reforms, including on tackling corruption. All 27 current EU states would have to ratify Montenegrin membership before it could join.
Still, Montenegro, once known for its warm ties with Russia, emerged as a frontrunner when the EU last month formed a group to draft its accession treaty.
On Friday, EU leaders will descend on the Adriatic resort of Tivat for a summit with Western Balkan counterparts in which they will discuss the country's integration.
"Montenegro is the most advanced candidate country in the accession negotiations," EU Enlargement Commissioner Marta Kos told Reuters.
"Strong leadership in the country has seen significant steps forward in terms of reforms, but there is still a lot of work to do. Some of the most pressing reforms are... on rule of law and the judiciary."
Montenegro has made big changes since it exited a state union with Serbia in 2006. Once heavily reliant on Russian cash, coastal real estate investment and tourists, Podgorica's relations with Moscow have soured.
In 2016, Montenegrin authorities accused Moscow of plotting with a group of Serbian ultranationalists to stage a coup, a charge the Kremlin denies.
Relations frayed further when Montenegro joined Nato in 2017.
In 2022, Montenegro enforced sanctions against Russia over the Ukraine war and Moscow declared it an enemy state.
Milo Djukanovic, who led Montenegro for three decades until 2023 as prime minister or president, said the country's split from Russia, a traditional Orthodox Christian and Slavic ally, marked a turning point.
[[nid:1455619]]
"Our primary motive is to take control of our own future, which will be European and Euro-Atlantic," he told Reuters.
Ordinary Montenegrins agree. In an April poll for the European Commission, more than 80 per cent believed EU membership would be positive for the country - a stark contrast to many other parts of Europe, where euroscepticism is on the rise.
"We have a future in Europe, we should not remain a part of the Balkan quagmire," said Novak, a 45-year-old physiotherapist from Podgorica, who declined to give his surname.
Montenegro has made progress. It has opened all 33 negotiating chapters and closed 14. In late 2025, it closed negotiations on agriculture and the free movement of capital.
Outstanding are the central chapters on the rule of law and fundamental rights. Under EU rules, no other chapters can be finalised until Montenegro fulfils the final benchmarks for these two areas.
Montenegro has launched an anti-corruption crackdown that led to high-profile arrests and convictions of several former top judges, prosecutors and police officials. But the number of convictions is low and analysts say problems persist.
Djukanovic and his Democratic Party of Socialists have faced accusations from critics and international watchdogs of state capture, corruption and ties with organised crime, which they deny.
Media freedoms are also under threat, the watchdogs said.
"Montenegro is yet to close the chapters relating to the judiciary, which tend to be among the biggest hurdles for aspiring EU members," said Mario Bikarski, senior Europe analyst at risk intelligence company Verisk Maplecroft.
"Even closer attention is likely to be given to these by the European Commission given the backsliding of several member states on the rule of law in recent years," he said.
Predrag Zenovic, Montenegro's chief EU negotiator, said he was confident the country would meet these demands in time.
"When the courts deliver a consolidated track record of results in... verdicts, that will be the final test," he said.
* The writer is from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar vows steady backing for high-quality investments
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim yesterday met AT&S chief executive officer Dr Michael Mertin to discuss the latest developments regarding the company's operations in Kulim, Kedah.
In a Facebook post, Anwar said the company currently employs around 2,700 workers in Kulim.
He added that Malaysia is one of AT&S's major production hubs, alongside Austria, China and India, reflecting global investors' continued confidence in the country's capabilities and competitiveness.
"AT&S is a global leader in high‑value integrated circuit (IC) substrates and printed circuit boards. It also develops advanced connectivity technologies supporting mobile devices, automotive, aerospace, medical, industrial and high‑performance computing for AI applications," he said.
Anwar, who is also Finance Minister, stressed that the government will continue to provide clear, consistent support for high‑quality investments to strengthen Malaysia's position in the global semiconductor value chain and create more high‑skilled jobs for Malaysians.
"The confidence shown by international investors like AT&S reflects our political stability, prudent economic management, and the clarity of the MADANI government policies," he said.– BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police rearrest teen Mercedes driver for murder probe after fatal Simpang Renggam crash
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, June 5 — The teenage driver previously remanded in connection with reckless and dangerous driving that resulted in the deaths of four family members in Simpang Renggam near Kluang has been rearrested for a murder probe.
Kluang district police chief ACP Bahrin Mohd Noh said the 19-year-old was initially remanded for two days beginning June 2 before the remand was extended by one day for investigations under Section 42(1) of the Road Transport Act 1987.
The suspect was granted police bail upon completion of the previous remand period, he said in a statement yesterday, adding that an application to remand him under Section 302 of the Penal Code would be made at the Kluang Court today.
Bahrin had earlier said that the June 1 crash at Kilometre 27, Jalan Renggam-Simpang Renggam resulted in the deaths of four members of a family travelling in a Toyota Vios, including a 10-year-old girl, who were confirmed dead at the scene.
